name: Aaron Abke
corePositions: Paul is the shadow side of Jesus
notablePatterns: Scripture citations are largely accurate, but interpretive frameworks and doctrinal characterizations carry heavy error loads.
Core thesis claims tend to be historically falsifiable and falsified.
Frequently employs Stratagem 11 (Induction with Concealed Counterexamples).

name: Wes Huff
corePositions: NT is the most accurately transmitted ancient document collection
Archaeological artifacts corroborate the New Testament
notablePatterns: Core claims about manuscript evidence are well-supported and within academic expertise.
Peripheral factual details (dates, attributions) carry most errors.
Uses cherry-picking of confirming archaeological evidence without addressing complications.
Popular-level lecture format leads to imprecise but not malicious claims.

name: Dr. James Tabor
notablePatterns: Strong Hebrew and Greek competence with legitimate textual observations.
Core thesis depends on systematic omission of counterevidence (Deuteronomy 16, feeding miracles).
Decontextualization of Jeremiah 7 (condemns Baal worship, not all sacrifice).
Academic credentials lend authority but thesis goes well beyond scholarly consensus.

name: Dan McClellan
corePositions: Wes Huff conflates textual criticism with historical criticism
Apologetics masquerades as scholarship on social media
Conservative evangelicals specialize in peripheral fields to avoid critical issues
Gospel-Tiberius comparison is fallacious due to vastly different evidence bases
99% consensus on first-century NT dating is false
notablePatterns: Strong command of scholarly consensus on NT dating, genre, and manuscript evidence.
Occasionally overstates the degree of scholarly unanimity on positions that favor his argument.
Editorial opinions and characterizations of motive sometimes presented as factual claims.
Accurately distinguishes textual criticism from historical criticism — a key methodological point.
